coredata.dc:title: Electro-deposited SnSe on ITO: A low-cost and high-performance counter electrode for DSSCs
coredata.dc:creator: Zatirostami, Ahmad
coredata.dc:description: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) are among the interesting generations of solar cells that require further research to achieve industrial production. Current platinum (Pt) counter electrodes used in DSSCs is expensive and should be replaced by cheaper materials. In this study, we propose the tin-selenide (SnSe) material prepared on ITO substrates by the electrodeposition method as a Pt replacement. Our prepared materials demonstrated excellent electrocatalytic behavior and charge transport with iodide/triiodide electrolyte that is confirmed by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Our best DSSC showed a VOC of 657 mV, a JSC of 11.8 mA/cm2, and an FF of 63% resulting in an efficiency of 4.9% that was slightly lower than the DSSC fabricated with Pt counter electrode. Therefore, our study shows that the low cost and earth-abundant SnSe prepared by the electrodeposition method is a suitable replacement for Pt in DSSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dye-sensitized solar cell
Counter electrode
Electrodeposition
Electrocatalyst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectrum of the electrodeposited SnSe layer on ITO.

(a–c) cross-sectional FESEM images of the electrodeposited SnSe samples with 5 min, 10 min and 15 min deposition time, (d) top view of the 10 min deposited sample and (e) EDS analysis of the 10 min SnSe sample.
(a) Absorption spectra and (b) Tauc plots of the electrodeposited samples with 5 min, 10 min, and 15 deposition times.
Cyclic voltammetry analysis of the prepared samples with 5 min, 10 min, and 15 min SnSe electrodeposition times and Pt CE.
BET isotherm of N2 adsorption-desorption of SnSe CEs.
EIS diagrams of the prepared CEs along with the equivalent circuit at inset.
Current-voltage characteristics of DSSCs fabricated from the prepared CEs and Pt CE.
Photovoltaic parameters of DSSCs fabricated from the prepared CEs and Pt CE.

head.ce:title: Electro-deposited SnSe on ITO: A low-cost and high-performance counter electrode for DSSCs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Science and Engineering, Sari Branch, Islamic Azad University, Sari, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Science and Engineering, Sari Branch, Islamic Azad University, Sari, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC) are among the interesting generations of solar cells that require further research to achieve industrial production. Current platinum (Pt) counter electrodes used in DSSCs is expensive and should be replaced by cheaper materials. In this study, we propose the tin-selenide (SnSe) material prepared on ITO substrates by the electrodeposition method as a Pt replacement. Our prepared materials demonstrated excellent electrocatalytic behavior and charge transport with iodide/triiodide electrolyte that is confirmed by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. Our best DSSC showed a VOC of 657 mV, a JSC of 11.8 mA/cm2, and an FF of 63% resulting in an efficiency of 4.9% that was slightly lower than the DSSC fabricated with Pt counter electrode. Therefore, our study shows that the low cost and earth-abundant SnSe prepared by the electrodeposition method is a suitable replacement for Pt in DSSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, renewable energies such as solar, wind and etc. [1,2] along with new methods of energy storage including super capacitors [3–7] and hydrogen production [8–10] are attracting significant attention as future energy supplies [11,12]. Among these resources, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) show to be promising due to their low cost and high-performance nature [13–20]. Current studies on DSSCs are focusing on further cost-effectiveness and durability, and new materials are suggested to replace the typical DSSC’s structure [21–26]. Among different components of DSSCs, the counter electrode (CE) accounts for increasing the cost due to the expensiveness of platinum (Pt) [27–29]. Recently, many researchers around the globe are suggesting new materials for replacement of Pt in DSSCs [30–34].

A good CE should possess a high conductivity and also a suitable catalytic activity so that it can catalyze the redox process in iodide/triiodide (I−/I3 −) electrolyte [35,36]. Alternative materials suggested with these properties include conductive polymers (polyaniline, polypyrrole, etc.) [30,37,38], carbon materials (carbon black, nanotubes, fullerenes, and graphene) [39–41] and various inorganic materials such as oxides (V2O5, WO3, etc.) [42–44], carbides (TiC, Fe3C, etc) [45–49] and selenides (MoSe2, NiSe, NiCoSe4, SnSe, etc.) [50–53] that have been used as CEs in DSSCs.

Among all of these materials, tin selenide (SnSe), which is earth-abundant and demonstrates excellent properties such as outstanding electrocatalytic performance and low bandgap [54], has not yet investigated up to its potentials as CE. There are only a few reports regarding the use of SnSe as CE. The first report is from Ramasamy et al. who could achieve an efficiency of 5.52% with SnSe CE prepared by drop-casting method [55]. In another report, Liu et al. prepared SnSe CEs through the spin coating method and reached 9.34% efficiency, which was a 5% improvement in comparison with their Pt cell with 8.9% [56]. In a more recent study, Kumar et al. used the screen-printing technique to fabricate SnSe CEs with 5.76% efficiency [57].

In this work, we prepare SnSe on ITO glass using the electrodeposition method to be used as CE in DSSCs. We analyze the photocatalytic and electrical properties of our proposed CE and investigate its photovoltaic performance. We believe that the electrodeposition method is a low-cost approach [58] to prepare SnSe CEs with suitable properties.

In this research, we used the electrodeposition method to prepare SnSe on indium-tin-oxide (ITO) substrates. For this purpose, we prepared an electrolyte by combining a 1.5 mM H2SeO3 (98%, Sigma-Aldrich) with 2.2 mM SnCl2 (analytical grade, Merck Co) solutions. We adjusted the pH of this electrolyte between 2 and 3 by a pH meter and diluted HCL solution. Using a standard three-electrode electrochemical cell with 1 cm × 1.5 cm ITO coated glass as the working electrode, A Pt sheet as the CE and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, we prepared SnSe thin films at 45 °C temperature by a constant voltage of −0.75 V applied with a potentiostat power supply. We investigated different deposition times in this study, but only the results of 0 min, 5 min, 10 min, and 15 min durations are presented. We also used commercial Pt coated FTO glass with ∼20 nm thickness purchased from Sharif Solar Co to compare with our prepared CEs.

In order to prepare the photoanode, we used TiO2 nanoparticles purchased from Merck with 99.7% purity and less than 25 nm particle size and a TiO2 Blocking layer deposited using RF sputtering from a ceramic target. For this purpose, we ultrasonically washed fluorine-doped-tin oxide (FTO)-coated glass in an ethanol and water solution for 30 min and dried them afterward using a high nitrogen gas pressure. We sputter-coated the FTO substrates with a 100 nm blocking layer of TiO2 by an RF power of 80 W and at 5 mbar pressure using an NSC sputtering system. In order to prepare the TiO2 paste, we dispersed the TiO2 nanoparticles with HPLC ethanol and vigorously stirred them on a magnetic stirrer for 60 min. Then, we added drops of Triton X100, Terpineol, and ethylene cellulose as binders and stirred the solution for another 60 min at room temperature. Later, we heated the solution to 80 °C temperature using an electrical heater and continued stirring until a suitable paste for deposition was acquired. We deposited the prepared paste on the blocking layer coated FTO substrates using the doctor-blade technique and sintered them at 500 °C for 30 min in an electrical furnace. We dyed the prepared photoanodes with the standard N719 dye solution for 24 h and washed the remaining dye molecules by ethanol. The photoanodes were then dried at room temperature.

All the photoanodes of our fabricated DSSCs were N719 dyed TiO2-pastes coated on TiO2 blocking layers on FTO substrates. We used ITO substrates coated with different durations of SnSe that were pierced with a small drill for electrolyte injection as the CEs. Surlyn spacers were sandwiched between the CEs and photoanodes and heated at 120 °C to melt the spacer and attain isolation. We injected the I−/I3 − electrolyte through the hole on the CE and sealed the hole with Surlyn so that the DSSC structure and isolation are completed.

We used an STOE STADI-P X-ray diffractometer (XRD) in the range of 2θ = 20 to 80° to analyze the crystalline structure of the samples. In order to acquire the morphological properties of the samples, we employed a Mira III Tescan field emission electron microscope (FESEM) with 15 kV operating voltage. The optical and bandgap analysis of the prepared materials were carried out by a 3648 Avantes UV–visible spectrometer in the wavelength range of 300–800 nm. To investigate the electrocatalytic behavior of the CEs, we used a high end, high current potentiostat/galvanostat (AUTOLAB 302 N). The porosity of the CEs was analyzed by a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analyzer (Belsorp mini II instrument). we also used a Sharif Solar PGE-18 electrochemical impedance spectrometer (EIS) to acquire the electrical parameters of the samples and a Sharif Solar current-voltage (I–V) tracer to find out its photovoltaic performance under an AM1.5 solar light simulator.

In order to investigate the crystallinity of the SnSe layers prepared by the electrodeposition method, we acquired their XRD spectra from them, which the result for 15 min SnSe CE is illustrated in Fig. 1 . In this figure, the observed peaks at 2θ = 24.9, 26.1, 29.0, 30.1, 30.7, 37.4, 41.1, 43.1, 47.0, 49.4, 51.7, 54.1, 57.5, 61.4, 64.4, 70.0, 71.5 and 76.1° can be attributed to the (201), (210), (011), (111), (400), (311), (102), (411), (302), (511), (402), (420), (502), (122), (602), (422), (702) and (413) crystalline planes which match the orthorhombic phase of Tin selenide with JCPDS 01-089-0236 card number. We can calculate the crystallite sizes of each plane using the Debye-Scherrer formula [59]:
D =
k λ
β cos
( θ )
Which
k
is a constant equal to 0.89,
λ
is the employed X-ray wavelength that is 1.54 Angstrom,
β
represents the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the peak, and
θ
is the diffraction angle. The average crystallite sizes of the planes were ∼54 nm, that is a suitable value in case of electrical conduction. Thus, we can deduce from these results that we have prepared an excellent crystalline SnSe layer with large crystallite sizes and no impurity phase.

Fig. 2 shows the FESEM images of the prepared CEs with Fig. 2a–c being the cross-sections of the electrodes with 5 min, 10 min, and 15 min deposition times, and Fig. 2d is the top view of 10 min CE. As we can see from these figures, the thickness of the SnSe layers increases by the increased deposition time, which is quite reasonable and could be expected. The thicknesses of layers with 5, 10, and 15 min depositions can be estimated to be around 1.2, 2.3, and 3.9 μm respectively. We also observe that the layers demonstrate a smooth and planar formation and are homogenous across their length. The top image (Fig. 2d) also shows the high quality of the prepared layer, which can facilitate the electron transport between the CE and electrolyte. Beside solar cells, such a structure has a great potential to be employed as a promising matrix for metacomposites with negative electromagnetic parameters [60–62]. The EDS analysis of the 10 min sample is shown in Fig. 2e from the figure, we can see that a high purity SnSe layer is formed with the stoichiometry seemingly preserved.

We employed UV–visible spectrometry to acquire the optical properties of the synthesized layers and to obtain their bandgaps. Fig. 3 a illustrates the absorption spectra of the prepared samples. As we can see from the figure, the absorbance of the SnSe layer deposited with 15 min duration is higher than the layer with 10 min deposition, which itself is higher than 5 min sample. The trend in the absorption spectra can correspond with direct relation to the thickness of SnSe layers. Using the Tacu formula [63]:
α h υ = A
(
h υ −
E g
)
n
in which
h υ
is the photon energy,
A
is a constant independent from energy and
n
is equal to 0.5 for direct bandgap semiconductors we can calculate the bandgaps of the prepared samples. Fig. 3b displays the Tauc plots of the samples prepared with 5, 10, and 15 min durations. The linear section of the diagram gives a bandgap of ∼1.38 eV for all the samples. This is because all the samples are made with the same materials and procedures, with only their thickness variations.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) is among the most important analyses to investigate the catalytic behavior of CEs, especially in DSSCs, and we used this analysis using our work, which its results are shown in Fig. 4 along with the CV curve of a Pt CE. In this research, we used a standard three-electrode cell for CV analysis with 1 cm × 1 cm SnSe coated ITO as the working electrode and recorded the CV curve with a scan rate of 50 mV/s. We also used the I−/I3 − (50 mM LiI, 10 mM I2, and 0.5 M LiClO4) as the electrolyte to simulate the DSSC working environment for the CE. In these diagrams, we witness two oxidation peaks and two reduction valleys which are o
2
I 3 −
↔ 3
I 2
+ 2
e _
and
3
I −
↔
I 3 −
+ 2
e _
reciprocal reactions showing the redox process in electrolyte. We can see that the reduction peaks have a higher amount in Pt CE in comparison with the SnSe CEs that shows Pt electrode’s better electrocatalytic performance. However, SnSe CEs are demonstrating a high electrocatalytic activity with I−/I3 − electrolyte, and their results are comparable to that of Pt CE. Among SnSe CEs, the 10 min deposited CE possesses the largest reduction peak, which demonstrate its highest electrocatalytic performance among SnSe CEs. This will result in a better charge transport behavior with the electrolyte species. The 10 min deposited SnSe CE also has the lowest peak to peak voltage, which shows its faster response to the oxidation/reduction with the electrolyte species in comparison with other CEs. The SnSe CEs show an almost the same peak to peak voltage and thus their current is determinant in their electrocatalytic behavior.

As we are aware, different electrocatalytic reactions in CEs cab be corresponded to their specific surface are [64]. The higher surface area increases the interactions with the electrolyte species and enhances the electrocatalytic activity. In order to analyze the specific surface areas of the prepared CEs we acquired their BET measurements and illustrated their results in Fig. 5 we can see that the CEs with 5, 10- and 15-min deposition time show respective specific surface areas of 39.3, 48.0 and 42.6 m2/g it is observed that the 10 min CE possesses the highest surface are that confirms its better electrocatalytic performance. The increase in the surface area from 5 min to 10 min samples are quite obvious because in the electrodeposition method, the initial layers are usually denser and their porosity is increased by time. On the other hand, we witness a decrease in porosity from 10 min to 15 min samples. Such a reduction in porosity has been previously observed by Donne and Dupont [65] that showed that during the initial stages, nucleation of crystallites on the surface increases the surface roughness, and hence, the electrode area. As the deposition continues, growth of existing crystallites becomes the more favorable process and the crystallites begin to merge, forming a solid film with a lower surface area.

To further analyze the charge transport behavior of the prepared CEs, we used the EIS measurement using SnSe and Pt CEs. For this purpose, we fabricated symmetric electrochemical cell with ITO/CE/(I−/I3 −)/CE/ITO structure so that the photoanode does not affect its properties. The EIS measurement was performed with 10 mV perturbation voltage between 0.1 Hz and 10 kHz frequencies in dark situation and the results are illustrated as Nyquist diagrams in Fig. 6 . In these diagrams, we see a single semicircle which corresponds to the charge transfer between CE and electrolyte. The other semicircles normally seen at midrange frequencies in DSSCs are not present in these diagrams due to the absence of charge transfer between the electrolyte and other materials in the symmetric cell. Furthermore, we can see the linear tails of the semicircles which are formed due to the Warburg impedances caused by the diffusion of electrolyte species. We can estimate the charge transfer resistance (RCT) of the CEs and electrolyte using the diameter of these semicircles. From the figure, we evaluate RCTs of 11.5, 8.1, 8.7, and 7.4 Ω for 5 min, 10 min, 15 min, and Pt CEs, respectively. The amount of RCT under the dark situation shows the difficulty of charge transfer, and thus the 10 min SnSe and Pt CEs have both better transport and higher electrocatalytic performance in comparison with the 5 min and 15 min CEs. From the figure, we also see that the 5 min deposited SnSe CE has the lowest amount of series resistance, which is due to its lower thickness. As we are aware, the X intercept of the Nyquist diagram is an evaluation of the series resistance in the system, and its lower values are favorable. Nevertheless, between the 5 min and 10 min samples, the first one has a lower series resistance, but on the other hand, the better electrocatalytic behavior and superior charge transfer of the 10 min sample make it a better CE for DSSCs.

In order to investigate the photovoltaic performance of the DSSCs, we acquired their current-voltage curves, which are shown in Fig. 7 . The resulting photovoltaic parameters are shown in Table 1 . In this figure, it is clear that the Pt CE has resulted in a better DSSC with 689 mV open-circuit voltage (VOC), 12.7 mA/cm2 short circuit current density (JSC) and 64% fill factor (FF) which have yielded an efficiency of 5.6%. we also see that the SnSe CEs performance is lower than the Pt CE, but they are still comparable to it with the 10 min deposited SnSe CE demonstrate an efficiency of 4.9%, which is only 14% behind its Pt CE counterpart. We see in Table 1 that this cell has a VOC of 657 mV, a JSC of 11.8 mA/cm2, and an FF of 63% that are the highest value among the DSSCs fabricated from SnSe CEs. The DSSCs fabricated from 5, to 15 min deposited SnSe show an almost similar photovoltaic property in case of the VOC and FF, but the higher current in the 10 min cell has increased its conversion efficiency. This higher current in the 10 min sample, could be expected from its higher electrocatalytic behavior and better charge transport witnessed in the CV and EIS measurements.

In this research, we proposed the electrodeposited SnSe layers on ITO coated glass to replace Pt as the CE in DSSCs. Although, our prepared CEs could not match the performance of Pt in DSSCs, but showed auspicious results so that our best SnSe cell had an efficiency of 4.9% which was only a 14% efficiency reduction in comparison to the Pt cell with 5.6% efficiency. The suitable performance of our prepared CEs is due to their excellent electrocatalytic behavior and good charge transport revealed by EIS and CV measurements. We showed that the low cost and earth-abundant SnSe material has the potential to replace Pt in DSSCs, and we believe that more studies can further improve it.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
